title: Consider Your Ways
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 03-17-2024 -PM- Consider Your Ways - Haggai 1 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Israel's History and the Setting of Haggai
subpoints: God brought Israel out of Egypt to be a people for himself; gave them the Promised Land, houses they didn't build, vineyards they didn't plant — but Israel forgot the God who delivered them

Judges 2:10-12: the generation after Joshua arose who did not know the Lord; seven cycles of sin → oppression → crying out → repentance → deliverance → sin again; 'every man did what was right in his own eyes'

Division of the kingdom; northern 10 tribes destroyed by Assyria in 722 BC; Judah taken by Babylon as God's purifying judgment; Jeremiah 29:10-11 (the promise in context: 70 years of captivity by design, then return)

By 520 BC: exiles have returned under Zerubbabel for 20 years; they had laid the temple foundation quickly but then stopped to build their own paneled houses; the temple has been abandoned for 20 years
Haggai: a single-issue prophet; unlike most OT prophets (who address idolatry, injustice), Haggai's single issue is the disobedient heart demonstrated in the slow progress of building the temple

outline.title: 1. The Excuse of 'Not Yet'
subpoints: Haggai 1:2 — 'this people says, the time has not come, the time that the Lord's house should be built' — a perfectly reasonable sounding excuse
There is a difference between a convenient moment to obey God and an intentional decision to obey God: the unplanned life will never afford a convenient moment to obey God
The people had family responsibilities, fields to plow, businesses to attend — they would get to the temple as soon as life afforded a moment; they were living without intentionality

By the time of Malachi, they were shaking their fist at God for not blessing them — offering the lame, divorcing their wives to marry richer women — and wondering why God was distant; a life of gradual, comfortable disobedience leads here

outline.title: 2. The Consequence of Disobedience
subpoints: Haggai 1:6 — 'You have sown much but bring in little; you eat but do not have enough; you drink but you are not filled; you clothe yourselves but no one is warm; and he who earns wages earns wages to put them into a bag with holes'
A life lived under the sun — without consideration of God's claims — is empty and meaningless; the world around us is not giving returns because they are not living under God's blessing
Haggai 1:9-11: God called for drought on the land and on the labor of their hands — not random misfortune but purposeful providential discipline
God's question: you ran to build your own houses — my house lies in ruins; how is that working out for you?

outline.title: 3. Why the Temple Mattered
subpoints: Not because God needed a building — 'the heavens of heavens cannot contain him' — but because the temple was where God's people experienced his presence
More than that, the temple pointed to the ultimate meeting point of God and man: Haggai 2:6-7 — 'the desire of the nations will come to this temple'
John 1:14: the Word became flesh and 'tabernacled' (eskenosen — same word for tabernacle) among us; Christ is the true temple, the ultimate meeting place of God and man
Abandoning the temple was abandoning anticipation of Christ; modern application: how we treat the church and the Word and the ordinances signals what we anticipate

outline.title: 4. The Response and the Exhortation
subpoints: Haggai 1:12-14: uniquely among OT prophets, the people actually listened and obeyed; Zerubbabel, Joshua, and the remnant feared the Lord and came and worked — within three weeks
God's message through Haggai: 'I am with you' (v.13) — the motivation for obedience is the presence of God
The refrain 'consider your ways' (vv. 5, 7): is your Christian life more about obedience or about excuses? 'It's just not the right time yet'

Diagnostic grid using vv. 6, 9: examine every area of life — work, financial planning, involvement with others, the church, marriage, parenting, use of technology, alcohol and drugs, friendships, eating — asking in each: obedience or excuses?

summary: A PM sermon on Haggai 1, part of a series on Haggai. After tracing Israel's history from the Exodus through the cycles of the book of Judges, the division of the kingdom, and the Babylonian captivity, Pastor Tito Lyro sets the scene: it is 520 BC, 20 years after the first exiles returned to Jerusalem. They laid the temple foundation but stopped building, settling into comfortable, paneled houses while the temple lay in ruins. Haggai is a single-issue prophet — his single issue is the disobedient heart shown in gradual complacency. The people's excuse: 'the time has not come yet.' God's response: you earn wages into a bag with holes; you eat but are not satisfied; you bring in little. A life lived without considering God's claims on it is empty and meaningless. The temple mattered not because God needed a building, but because it was the place of his presence and pointed forward to Christ (Haggai 2:6-7; John 1:14). Uniquely, the people responded immediately and got back to work. The sermon closes with the prophet's repeated exhortation — 'consider your ways' — applied to every area of life through a diagnostic grid: is this area of my life characterized by obedience or by excuses?
transcript:
## Introduction: Israel's History and the Setting of Haggai

We're going to turn to the book of Haggai — perhaps not the most familiar book in your Bible. If you have trouble finding it, go to Matthew and go back three books. It is the third-to-last book of the Old Testament, which also happens to be the order in which those last three books were written: Haggai, Zechariah, Malachi.

Haggai chapter 1. This is the word of our Lord:

*In the second year of King Darius, in the sixth month, on the first day of the month, the word of the LORD came by Haggai the prophet to Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and to Joshua the son of Jehozadak, the high priest, saying, 'Thus speaks the LORD of hosts, saying: This people says, "The time has not come, the time that the LORD's house should be built."' Then the word of the LORD came by Haggai the prophet, saying, 'Is it time for you yourselves to dwell in your paneled houses, and this temple to lie in ruins?' Now therefore, thus says the LORD of hosts: 'Consider your ways! You have sown much, and bring in little; you eat, but do not have enough; you drink, but you are not filled with drink; you clothe yourselves, but no one is warm; and he who earns wages, earns wages to put into a bag with holes.' Thus says the LORD of hosts: 'Consider your ways! Go up to the mountains and bring wood and build the temple, that I may take pleasure in it and be glorified,' says the LORD.*

Then the people obeyed the voice of the Lord their God, and feared the presence of the Lord. And Haggai the Lord's messenger spoke the Lord's message to the people: 'I am with you, says the LORD.'

The Christian life is a life of transformation — each day becoming more like our Lord Jesus Christ. Haggai will help us in that transformation journey.

You notice the refrain in this chapter: at least twice the prophet, or God through the prophet, calls the people to *consider their ways.* That is what I want us to do this afternoon.

## Israel's History

God brought Israel out of Egypt to be a people for himself. He gave them the Promised Land — houses they did not build, vineyards they did not plant, water they did not draw. He gave them a system through which they would have access to him. But as soon as Israel took possession of most of the land, Israel forgot the God who had delivered them.

Judges 2:10-12: *There arose another generation after them who did not know the LORD, nor the work which he had done for Israel. Then the children of Israel did evil in the sight of the LORD and served the Baals; and they forsook the LORD God of their fathers.* After everything God had done — everything Joshua witnessed, everything the previous generation saw — they did such a poor job of passing the faith on that the next generation did not know God.

The book of Judges then gives us seven cycles: sin → oppression → crying out → repentance → deliverance → sin again. And the refrain: 'In those days there was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes.'

God's repeated deliverances were met with rejection — culminating in a demand for a king like the other nations (1 Samuel 8). This led eventually to the division of the kingdom. The northern ten tribes established their own kingdom, built golden calves at Dan and Bethel so people could worship the God of the Bible at convenient shrines — and 200 years later, the Assyrians destroyed them. 722 BC. The northern kingdom was never re-established.

God remained patient with Judah — until the best thing for them was to hand them over to Babylon for a season of purification. Even then it was not final rejection: Jeremiah told the captives in Babylon that after 70 years God would visit them, perform his good word toward them, and cause them to return. *'For I know the plans I have for you,' declares the LORD, 'plans of peace and not of evil, to give you a future and a hope.'* That verse applies directly to a captive — someone in the middle of where Iraq is today, there because of their sins; those are the people it is written to.

By 520 BC, exiles had returned to Jerusalem under Zerubbabel for about 20 years. They had laid the temple foundation right away — but then stopped. They built their own houses, remodeled their houses, improved their remodels, and the temple sat on a cold foundation for two decades.

Haggai is unique among the prophets. Most — Isaiah, Jeremiah, the others — are dealing with idolatry, adultery, injustice. Haggai has a single issue: the disobedient heart of the people demonstrated in the slow progress of building the temple.

## 1. The Excuse: 'The Time Has Not Come'

Verse 2: *'This people says, "The time has not come, the time that the LORD's house should be built."'*

That sounds so reasonable. They had family responsibilities. Fields to plow. Businesses to attend. They were going to get to the temple as soon as life afforded a convenient moment.

The problem is that *the unplanned life will never afford a convenient moment to obey God.* The unplanned life is never going to offer a convenient window for what God is calling you to do. These people were lacking intentionality — living without direction, without plan, without resolve. They seem to have assumed that transformation, growth, and obedience would just happen on their own.

Slow descent into complacency looks like this: you come back from a conference energized with plans and commitments. The days go by. The weeks pass. And slowly, quietly, that re-invigoration fades, and you settle back into the life of comfortable disobedience that was there before.

That is what's happening in Haggai. Life just got in the way of obeying God. They had a reasonable explanation — it just wasn't the time. Have you ever found yourself thinking: 'God is telling me to do this, but it's just not convenient right now. It's not the right battle to pick. I'll do it eventually.' And eventually becomes never.

By the time of Malachi, those same people's descendants were shaking their fists at God for not blessing them — even as they were offering the lame animals and divorcing their wives to marry richer women. That's where gradual, unintentional disobedience leads.

## 2. The Consequence

God's question to them, in verse 4: 'Is it time for you to dwell in your paneled houses, while this temple lies in ruins?' And then verse 6: *You have sown much and bring in little; you eat but do not have enough; you drink but you are not filled; you clothe yourselves but no one is warm; and he who earns wages earns wages to put into a bag with holes.*

A life lived under the sun — without considering God's claims on us — is empty and meaningless. Everything you pour into it disappears. You think you're living, but the return isn't there. Why? God says in verses 9-11: *Because of my house that is in ruins, while every one of you runs to his own house. Therefore the heavens above you withhold the dew, and the earth withholds its fruit... I called for a drought on the land.* This was not random misfortune. This was purposeful providential discipline — God withdrawing what he had given so they would notice and return.

## 3. Why the Temple Mattered

God is not jealous of a building — the heavens of heavens cannot contain him. But the temple was the place where his people experienced his presence. And more than that, the temple was pointing forward to the ultimate meeting point of God and man.

Look at Haggai 2:6-7: *'For thus says the LORD of hosts: "Once more — it is a little while — I will shake heaven and earth, the sea and dry land; and I will shake all nations, and they shall come to the Desire of All Nations, and I will fill this temple with glory."'* The desire of the nations is coming to this temple. Who is that? John 1:14: the Word became flesh and *tabernacled* among us — the same word as the tabernacle. He pitched his tent among us. The temple was pointing to Christ, the ultimate meeting place of God and man.

So abandoning the temple was abandoning the anticipation of Christ.

## 4. The Response: Obedience

Haggai is unique in another way: the people actually listened. Verses 12-14: Zerubbabel, Joshua, and all the remnant of the people obeyed the voice of the Lord their God. They feared the presence of the Lord. Three weeks later — within three weeks of the prophetic word — they were back building. That is like lightning speed in the Old Testament. The people of God respond in obedience, and God immediately says: *'I am with you.'*

Obedience is the life. Not something secondary to living life — it is living life.

## The Exhortation: Consider Your Ways

The prophet's refrain: *Consider your ways.* Is your Christian life more about obedience to what God commands or about excuses for why you don't have to, or why not yet, or why you'll get to it eventually?

Use verses 6 and 9 as a diagnostic grid:

- Is the way you work about obedience to the Son of God or about excuses?
- Is the way you plan your life — with intentionality — about obedience or about excuses?
- Is the way you budget and use your resources about obedience or about excuses?
- Is your involvement in the lives of other people about obedience or about excuses?
- Is your involvement in the body of Christ — this church — about obedience or about excuses?
- Is the way you love your spouse about obedience or about excuses?
- Is the way you are raising your children about obedience or about excuses?
- Is the way you honor and obey your parents about obedience or about excuses?
- Is the way you use your computer, tablet, and phone about obedience or about excuses?
- Is the way you relate to alcohol and drugs about obedience or about excuses?
- Is the way you carry out your friendships about obedience or about excuses?
- Is the way you eat about obedience or about excuses?

God through the prophet calls us: consider your ways. As we do that — as we turn from comfortable, gradual, unintentional disobedience to intentional following of the Lord Jesus — we are transformed. The Christian life is not about celebrating the times. It is about transforming them as we follow Christ.

Father, you are a God who speaks — and you speak the same message across the ages, even through this ancient prophet. Help us to consider our ways. Help us to repent of delayed obedience and to pursue you intentionally. In Jesus' name, amen.
